items.q: How do you say 'I will drink coffee tomorrow' in German?
items.why: Students translate 'will' as 'will'. The German 'will' (from wollen) means 'want', not future. Future = WERDEN + infinitive. Also, German often uses present + time word ('Ich trinke morgen Kaffee') for near future.

items.q: How do you say 'my sister' in German?
items.why: Students forget possessive endings. Possessives (mein, dein, sein, ihr, unser, euer) take the SAME ENDINGS as ein/eine. Schwester is feminine = meine. Mein Bruder (masc), meine Schwester (fem), mein Kind (neut), meine Eltern (pl).

A devastating faux ami. Bekommen looks like 'become' but means RECEIVE. 'Ich bekomme ein Geschenk' = I receive a present. To become = WERDEN. Confusion: 'I became a present' instead of 'I received a present'.

items.q: Which is the Präteritum of 'kommen' (3rd person sg)?
items.why: It's kam. “kommt” is the classic near-miss here; knowing why it's wrong is worth as much as knowing the right answer.

items.q: When is Tag der Deutschen Einheit?
items.why: The answer examiners want is: 3 October. “1 May” is the distractor this question was built around — if it tempted you, that's the exact gap to close.

items.q: In what year was Germany reunified?
items.why: The real figure is 1990. The wrong options cluster deliberately close — anchor the exact number to one concrete fact so it can't drift.
